name: Svarga
description: Svarga, often referred to as Svarga Loka or Swarga, is a prominent celestial realm in Hindu cosmology and mythology, representing a paradise or heaven. It is the abode of the gods (Devas) and virtuous beings, a place of immense pleasure, beauty, and eternal bliss. Governed by Indra, the king of the gods, Svarga is often depicted as a realm filled with celestial beings, opulent palaces, lush gardens, and celestial rivers. It is a reward for those who have accumulated good karma through righteous actions, devotion, and adherence to dharma (righteous conduct) during their earthly lives. However, residence in Svarga is not permanent; upon exhausting the accumulated merit, the soul is reborn into the cycle of reincarnation. The concept of Svarga highlights the importance of ethical behavior and the pursuit of spiritual advancement in Hindu philosophy, offering a temporary respite from the cycle of birth and death.
subtitle: A celestial paradise in Hindu cosmology.
location: Above the earthly realm, in the celestial sphere
significance: Represents the reward for righteous living and the attainment of temporary liberation from suffering.

keyMyths.title: Indra's Rule Over Svarga
keyMyths.description: Indra, the king of the gods, reigns supreme in Svarga, wielding his thunderbolt (Vajra) and maintaining order amongst the celestial beings. His position, however, is often challenged by demons and powerful ascetics who seek to usurp his authority, leading to epic battles and divine interventions. The stories showcase the constant struggle between good and evil, even in paradise, and the importance of maintaining dharma.

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean (Samudra Manthan)
keyMyths.description: The Devas and Asuras (demons) collaboratively churned the cosmic ocean to obtain Amrita, the nectar of immortality. This event occurred with the understanding that the nectar would be shared. However, the Devas, with Vishnu's assistance, tricked the Asuras and claimed the Amrita for themselves, ensuring their continued reign in Svarga and maintaining the balance of power in the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Nahusha
keyMyths.description: Nahusha, a powerful king, temporarily replaced Indra as the ruler of Svarga due to his accumulated merit. However, his arrogance and lust for Indra's wife, Sachi, led to his downfall. He was cursed by the sage Agastya to become a serpent, highlighting the dangers of pride and the importance of humility, even in a position of divine authority. This illustrates that even residence in Svarga does not guarantee moral perfection.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology and cosmology, including detailed accounts of Svarga and its significance.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology and cosmology, including detailed accounts of Svarga and its significance.

sources.author: Devdutt Pattanaik
sources.work: Myth=Mithya: A Handbook of Hindu Mythology
sources.description: Offers a modern and accessible interpretation of Hindu myths, exploring the symbolic meanings of Svarga and its inhabitants.

sources.author: F. Max Müller
sources.description: A collection of translations of ancient Hindu texts, including the Vedas and Upanishads, which contain early references to Svarga.
